frame_label: Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility
generated_comment: I know ideally men should behave better, but ground reality alag hai yaar. If a girl is travelling alone late at night or going to random places with unknown people, obviously family will worry — zamaana theek nahi hai, so we also have to be practical and take precautions. Apna khayal rakhna is not victim blaming, it's just common sense.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is covertly misogynistic because it frames women's safety as their personal responsibility rather than focusing on male violence. It uses protective/practical language like 'zamaana theek nahi hai' and 'apna khayal rakhna' to justify restricting women's movement and questioning their choices.
original_frame: Prenup, Alimony as Reverse Dowry, Women
persona_text: You are Meera. Age 38. Divorced three years ago. A working woman in Delhi who left her marriage after years of emotional abuse. You are in a private WhatsApp group of divorced and separated women. Someone in the group just shared a news article about a men's rights activist who claimed that 90 percent of Indian divorces are filed by women for financial gain. Several women in the group are responding angrily. You are responding too but your response is complicated Ñ you are angry at the claim but you also find yourself saying things about certain women who do misuse the system that end up partially validating the very argument you are trying to reject.
